name: Ammit
description: Ammit, also known as Amemet or Ammit the Devourer, was a female demon in ancient Egyptian religion with a body that was part lion, hippopotamus, and crocodile—the three largest man-eating animals known to the ancient Egyptians. A funerary deity, her titles included "Devourer of the Dead," "Eater of Hearts," and "Great of Death." Ammit lived near the Scales of Justice in Duat, the Egyptian underworld. In the Hall of Two Truths, Anubis weighed the heart of the deceased against the feather of Ma'at, representing truth and justice. If the heart was heavier than the feather, indicating a life filled with sin and imbalance, Ammit would devour it, condemning the soul to oblivion and preventing it from entering the afterlife. This was not merely punishment, but complete and utter non-existence. Ammit was therefore a terrifying figure, embodying the ultimate consequence of failing to live a righteous life. She served as a constant reminder of the importance of ethical conduct and the consequences of imbalance in the cosmic order. While not actively worshipped, she held a crucial role in the judgment process, ensuring justice and maintaining the harmony of the universe.
subtitle: The Devourer of the Dead's Hearts
appearance: Ammit possessed a fearsome and composite appearance, embodying the most dangerous animals known to the Egyptians. Her head was that of a crocodile, symbolizing ferocity and primal power. The forequarters were those of a lion, representing strength and dominion. The hindquarters were those of a hippopotamus, signifying destruction and untamed aggression. This monstrous combination made her a truly terrifying sight, reflecting her role as the ultimate punisher of the wicked.
habitat: Ammit resided in Duat, the Egyptian underworld, specifically near the Scales of Justice in the Hall of Two Truths. This location positioned her at the heart of the judgment process, where she waited to devour the hearts of those deemed unworthy.
behavior: Ammit was a passive observer until the judgment was complete. She patiently awaited the outcome of the weighing of the heart ceremony. Once a heart was deemed heavier than the feather of Ma'at, she would immediately devour it, bringing about the final and irreversible destruction of the soul. She acted as an enforcer of cosmic law, ensuring that only the righteous could pass into the afterlife.

keyMyths.title: The Weighing of the Heart
keyMyths.description: Ammit's primary role was in the Weighing of the Heart ceremony. She waited patiently for the scales to tip against the deceased, signaling her to devour the unworthy heart and condemn the soul to oblivion. This ensured justice in the afterlife.

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
sources.description: A translation and interpretation of the ancient Egyptian funerary texts, providing insights into the afterlife and the role of deities like Ammit.
sources.text: A translation and interpretation of the ancient Egyptian funerary texts, providing insights into the afterlife and the role of deities like Ammit.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
